title: Haemophilia A and Haemophilia B
body_html:
Haemophilia is a X-linked recessive disorder of coagulation. Up to 30% of patients have no family history of the condition. Haemophilia A is due to a deficiency of factor VIII whilst in haemophilia B (Christmas disease) there is a lack of factor IX.

Inherited coagulation disorders are genetic conditions characterised by deficiencies or dysfunctions of clotting factors, leading to an increased risk of bleeding. The most clinically important are Haemophilia A, Haemophilia B, and von Willebrand disease (vWD).

Features
  • Haemoarthroses
  • Haematomas
  • Prolonged bleeding after surgery or trauma

Blood tests
  • Prolonged APTT
  • Bleeding time, thrombin time, prothrombin time normal
  • Low clotting factor levels (factor VIII in Haemophilia A and factor IX in haemophilia B)

Up to 10-15% of patients with haemophilia A develop antibodies to factor VIII treatment

Inheritance
X-linked recessive inheritance pattern. Click on image to enlarge

The other scenario is an affected father and carrier mother having offspring. In this case 50% of males will have haemophilia with 50% unaffected, 50% of females will have haemophilia and the other 50% of females will be carriers. Click on image to enlarge
Comparison of Haemophilia A and Haemophilia B

FeatureHaemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency)Haemophilia B (Factor IX deficiency)
GeneticsX-linked recessiveX-linked recessive
Incidence1 in 5,000 male births1 in 30,000 male births
Factor deficiencyFactor VIIIFactor IX
Clinical featuresHaemarthroses, deep muscle bleeds, prolonged bleeding after trauma/surgerySimilar to Haemophilia A (joint and muscle bleeds, prolonged bleeding)
InvestigationsProlonged APTT, normal PT, reduced FVIII activityProlonged APTT, normal PT, reduced FIX activity
ManagementRecombinant factor VIII replacement; Desmopressin (DDAVP) useful in mild diseaseRecombinant factor IX replacement (DDAVP not effective)
5
